title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: It's generally accepted that eating a healthy diet can help you feel better and stronger, both during and after treatment.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Physical activities like walking, riding a bicycle or swimming can help you feel better and less tired. Talk to your doctor before starting an exercise routine.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Knowing you have cancer can be overwhelming. You may have worries about your condition and how sick you could get, how it will affect you and your family, treatments and hospital stays, medical bills, and your job.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: There are ways to find support for you. Remember that your doctor and healthcare team are there to answer any questions you have. The following sources of support can help you, social workers, faith leaders, counselors, and support groups.

title: ANH19229 Managing Blood Cancers
text_en: Talk to your doctor if you have any questions about your treatment plan, medications, or lifestyle changes to help you manage your blood cancer.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: You or someone you care about may have been diagnosed with CKD Anemia or Anemia Due to Chronic Kidney Disease. This video will help you understand more about CKD Anemia and how it affects your body.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: Anemia happens when you don't have enough red blood cells.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: Red blood cells are made in the center of bones called bone marrow.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: In red blood cells, iron is needed to make a protein that carries oxygen called hemoglobin.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: Red blood cells flow through the bloodstream to the lungs to pick up oxygen. As blood flows through the lungs, oxygen moves from air sacs into the blood. Then oxygen attaches to the hemoglobin in red blood cells.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: These red blood cells deliver oxygen to cells throughout the body. Cells need oxygen to function and survive.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: In Anemia, there are not enough healthy red blood cells to deliver oxygen to the body. This means that organs and tissues may not work as well as they should.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: While many conditions may cause anemia, it is often caused by chronic kidney disease. The kidneys make a hormone called erythropoyetin or EPO. Bone marrow uses EPO to make red blood cells.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: Red kidney disease damages the kidneys so they don't make enough EPO. As a result, bone marrow makes less red blood cells causing anemia.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: Symptoms of CKD anemia may include feeling tired or weak, pale skin color, headaches, problems concentrating or thinking, loss of appetite, chest pain, a fast heartbeat, feeling light-headed when you stand up, and shortness of breath with activity.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: A doctor may test a sample of your blood to see if you have CKD anemia. For example, a complete blood count test can measure the amount of hemoglobin and red blood cells in a sample.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: A normal hemoglobin range in healthy adults is 13.8 to 17.2 for men and 12.1 to 15.1 for women. A low hemoglobin level in chronic kidney disease is below 13 for men and below 12 for women. Your doctor may perform other tests to evaluate CKD anemia.

title: ANH20232 Understanding CKD Anemia
text_en: If you have questions about CKD anemia, when to start treatment, or any medications you have been prescribed, talk to your doctor. It is important to take your medications as directed and report any side effects you have.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: You or someone you care about may have been diagnosed with Anemia as a result of chronic kidney disease or CKD. This video will help you understand some of the available treatment options for CKD

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: Anemia means your blood does not have enough healthy red blood cells to deliver oxygen to the body.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: Anemia can result from chronic kidney disease. Normally, the kidneys make a hormone called erythropoyetin. This hormone helps bone marrow make new red blood cells. When the kidneys are damaged, they don't make enough of this hormone. As a result, bone marrow doesn't make enough red blood cells causing CKD Anemia. This means that organs and tissues may not work as well as they should.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: Anemia treatment options for CKD Anemia may include one or more of the following. An erythropoyetin stimulating agent, iron, and the blood transfusion.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: An erythropoyetin stimulating agent or ESA helps your bone marrow make new red blood cells.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: Another treatment option is iron. For Dr. May prescribed oral iron, this means pills taken by mouth. Or you may receive intravenous iron therapy. In this procedure, iron is delivered through an IV to increase the amount of iron in your

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: For severe anemia, you may need a blood transfusion. This means you will be given red blood cells and iron through an IV. It will quickly increase the amount of these substances in your blood.

title: ANH20233 Treatment Options for CKD Anemia
text_en: If you have questions about treatments for CKD Anemia or any medications you have been prescribed, talk to your doctor. It is important to take your medications as directed and report any side effects you have.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Cyclecell anemia is an inherited blood disease that affects your red blood cells. It's one of the most common and usually most severe forms of a group of red blood cell disorders called sickle cell disease.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: In order to survive, your body needs red blood cells to supply oxygen to its tissues. Red blood cells are made inside the center of your bones called bone marrow. Their immature cells called blood stem cells can divide to make all types of blood cells, including red blood cells.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Normal red blood cells are disc shape. Each of them contains protein molecules called hemoglobin that carry oxygen. Round flexible red blood cells flow easily through your bloodstream and pick up oxygen in your lungs.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: In tiny air sacs in the lungs, called alveoli, oxygen moves into the blood and attaches to the hemoglobin in red blood cells. Then, the red blood cells deliver oxygen to cells throughout the body.
